title: Introduction to kardl
text: kardl package R tool estimating symmetric asymmetric Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) Nonlinear ARDL (NARDL) models, designed econometricians researchers analyzing cointegration dynamic relationships time series data. offers flexible model specifications, allowing users include deterministic variables, asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics, trend components. package supports customizable lag structures, model selection criteria (AIC, BIC, AICc, HQ), parallel processing computational efficiency. Key features include: Flexible Formula Specification: Use asymmetric(), lasymmetric(), sasymmetric() model asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics, deterministic() dummy variables. Lag Optimization: Choose automatic lag selection ("quick", "grid", "grid_custom") user-defined lags. Dynamic Analysis: Compute long-run coefficients, perform cointegration tests (PSS F, PSS t, Narayan), ECM estimation. vignette demonstrates use kardl() function estimate asymmetric ARDL model, perform diagnostic tests, visualize results, using economic data Turkey.

title: Introduction to kardl
text: kardl R can easily installed CRAN repository: Alternatively, can use devtools package load directly GitHub: Load package:
code: install.packages("kardl") library(kardl) # Install required packages install.packages(c(   "stats", "msm", "lmtest", "nlWaldTest", "car", "strucchange",   "utils", "ggplot2" )) # Install kardl from GitHub install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("karamelikli/kardl") library(kardl)

title: Introduction to kardl
text: kardl package implements several methodological extensions improvements ARDL/NARDL modelling go beyond standard implementations available R software: different_asym_lag option: Enables different lag orders positive negative partial sum decompositions NARDL models. provides greater flexibility common symmetric lag restriction used existing packages. Narayan cointegration test (narayan()): dedicated small-sample bounds test (Narayan, 2005) automatic handling critical values cases II–V. test exists literature, seamless integration full ARDL/NARDL workflow unique R. Symmetry tests (symmetrytest()): Comprehensive Wald tests short-run long-run symmetry NARDL models. Bootstrap confidence intervals dynamic multipliers: Robust uncertainty quantification around short-run, long-run, impact multipliers resampling methods, fully integrated asymmetric multiplier estimation. Additional advanced capabilities include highly flexible formula parsing mixed symmetric/asymmetric regressors, multiple model selection criteria (AIC, BIC, AICc, HQ), parallel processing support, extensive post-estimation tools tailored asymmetric analysis. features make kardl particularly suitable researchers needing fine-grained control asymmetric dynamics small-sample inference.

title: Introduction to kardl
text: define model formula using R’s formula syntax, incorporating asymmetric effects deterministic variables. use asymmetric() variables short- long-run asymmetry, lasymmetric() long-run asymmetry, sasymmetric() short-run asymmetry, deterministic() fixed dummy variables. trend term includes linear time trend model. Indeed, formula syntax flexible, allowing various combinations asymmetric deterministic variables. following variations formula equivalent yield model specification:
code: # Define the model formula my_formula <- DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers +   asymmetric(PetrolPrice + drivers) + deterministic(law) + trend same_formula <- y ~ asymmetric(x1) +   sasymmetric(x2 + x3) +   lasymmetric(x4 + x5) +   deterministic(dummy1) + trend same_formula <- y ~ asymmetric(x1) +   sasymmetric(x2 + x3) +   lasymmetric(x4 + x5) +   deterministic(dummy1) + trend same_formula <- y ~ asym(x1) + sasym(x2 + x3) + lasym(x4 + x5) +   det(dummy1) + trend same_formula <- y ~ a(x1) + s(x2 + x3) + l(x4 + x5) + d(dummy1) + trend

title: Introduction to kardl
text: lag_criteria component contains lag combinations criterion values. visualize compare model selection criteria (AIC, BIC, HQ).
code: library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(ggplot2) # Convert lag_criteria to a data frame lag_criteria <- as.data.frame(kardl_extract(kardl_model, "lag_criteria")) lag_criteria <- lag_criteria |> mutate(across(c(AIC, BIC, HQ), as.numeric))  # Pivot to long format lag_criteria_long <- lag_criteria |>   select(-AICc) |>   pivot_longer(     cols = c(AIC, BIC, HQ),     names_to = "Criteria",     values_to = "Value"   )  # Find minimum values min_values <- lag_criteria_long |>   group_by(Criteria) |>   slice_min(order_by = Value) |>   ungroup()  # Plot ggplot(   lag_criteria_long,   aes(x = lag, y = Value, color = Criteria, group = Criteria) ) +   geom_line() +   geom_point(     data = min_values, aes(x = lag, y = Value),     color = "red", size = 3, shape = 8   ) +   geom_text(     data = min_values, aes(x = lag, y = Value, label = lag),     vjust = 1.5, color = "black", size = 3.5   ) +   labs(     title = "Lag Criteria Comparison",     x = "Lag Configuration",     y = "Criteria Value"   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Introduction to kardl
text: symmetrytest() function performs Wald tests assess short- long-run asymmetry model. Summary symmetry test provides detailed results long-run short-run asymmetry tests, including F-values, p-values, hypotheses, test decisions.
code: ast <- Seatbelts |>   kardl(     DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asymmetric(PetrolPrice + drivers) +       deterministic(law) + trend,     mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0, 1),     data = _   ) |>   symmetrytest() ast ##  ## KARDL Symmetry Test Results ## Symmetry Test Results - Long-run: ## ======================= ##             Df Sum of Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F) ## PetrolPrice  1     2.663   2.663   0.020 0.8877 ## drivers      1   175.149 175.149   1.316 0.2530 ##  ## Symmetry Test Results - Short-run: ## ======================= ##             Df Sum of Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F) ## PetrolPrice  1     2.649   2.649  0.0199 0.8880 ## drivers      1    35.235  35.235  0.2647 0.6076 # Summary of symmetry test summary(ast) ##  ## Long-run symmetry tests ## ----------------------- ##                   F  Pr(>F) ## PetrolPrice 0.02001 0.88768 ## drivers     1.31595 0.25295 ##  ## Hypotheses and decisions: ##  ## Variable: PetrolPrice  ## H0: - Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS)/Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = - Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG)/Coef(L1.DriversKilled)  ## H1: At least one coefficient differs from zero.  ## Decision: Fail to Reject H0 at 5% level. Indicating long-run symmetry for variable PetrolPrice.  ##  ## Variable: drivers  ## H0: - Coef(L1.drivers_POS)/Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = - Coef(L1.drivers_NEG)/Coef(L1.DriversKilled)  ## H1: At least one coefficient differs from zero.  ## Decision: Fail to Reject H0 at 5% level. Indicating long-run symmetry for variable drivers.  ##  ##  ## Short-run symmetry tests ## ------------------------ ##                   F  Pr(>F) ## PetrolPrice 0.01990 0.88797 ## drivers     0.26473 0.60756 ##  ## Hypotheses and decisions: ##  ## Variable: PetrolPrice  ## H0: Coef(L0.d.PetrolPrice_POS) + Coef(L1.d.PetrolPrice_POS) + Coef(L2.d.PetrolPrice_POS) = Coef(L0.d.PetrolPrice_NEG) + Coef(L1.d.PetrolPrice_NEG) + Coef(L2.d.PetrolPrice_NEG) + Coef(L3.d.PetrolPrice_NEG)  ## H1: Coef(L0.d.PetrolPrice_POS) + Coef(L1.d.PetrolPrice_POS) + Coef(L2.d.PetrolPrice_POS) ≠ Coef(L0.d.PetrolPrice_NEG) + Coef(L1.d.PetrolPrice_NEG) + Coef(L2.d.PetrolPrice_NEG) + Coef(L3.d.PetrolPrice_NEG)  ## Decision: Fail to Reject H0 at 5% level. Indicating short-run symmetry for variable PetrolPrice.  ##  ## Variable: drivers  ## H0: Coef(L0.d.drivers_POS) = Coef(L0.d.drivers_NEG) + Coef(L1.d.drivers_NEG)  ## H1: Coef(L0.d.drivers_POS) ≠ Coef(L0.d.drivers_NEG) + Coef(L1.d.drivers_NEG)  ## Decision: Fail to Reject H0 at 5% level. Indicating short-run symmetry for variable drivers.

title: Introduction to kardl
text: pssf() function tests cointegration using Pesaran, Shin, Smith F Bound test. Summary PSS F Bound test provides detailed information test statistic, critical values, hypotheses, decision regarding cointegration.
code: test_result <- kardl_model |> pssf(case = 3, signif_level = "0.05") test_result ##  ##  Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds F-test for cointegration ##  ## data:  kardl_model ## F = 32.345 ## alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists summary(test_result) ##  ## ======================================== ## KARDL Cointegration Test Results ## ======================================== ##  ##  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 5% level) ##  ##  Test Statistic: ##   F: 32.3445539 ##  ##  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): ##           L    U ##   10%  3.03 4.06 ##   5%   3.47 4.57 ##   2.5% 3.89 5.07 ##   1%   4.40 5.72 ##  ##  ##  Comparison: ##   At the 5% significance level, F (32.3445539) exceeds the upper bound (4.57). ##   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. ##   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). ##  ##  Hypotheses: ## H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG) = Coef(L1.drivers_POS) = Coef(L1.drivers_NEG) = 0  ## H1: Not all of Coef(L1.DriversKilled), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG), Coef(L1.drivers_POS), Coef(L1.drivers_NEG) are zero.  ##  ##  Model Details: ##   Number of regressors (k): 4 ##   Case: V  ##  ## ========================================

text: psst() function tests significance lagged dependent variable’s coefficient. Summary PSS t Bound test provides detailed information test statistic, critical values, hypotheses, decision regarding cointegration.
code: test_result <- kardl_model |> psst(case = 3, signif_level = "0.05") test_result ##  ##  Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds t-test for cointegration ##  ## data:  model ## t = -12.63 ## alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists summary(test_result) ##  ## ======================================== ## KARDL Cointegration Test Results ## ======================================== ##  ##  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 5% level) ##  ##  Test Statistic: ##   t: -12.6296459 ##  ##  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): ##            L     U ##   10%  -3.13 -4.04 ##   5%   -3.41 -4.36 ##   2.5% -3.65 -4.62 ##   1%   -3.96 -4.96 ##  ##  ##  Comparison: ##   At the 5% significance level, t (12.6296459) exceeds the upper bound (4.36). ##   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. ##   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). ##  ##  Hypotheses: ## H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = 0  ## H1: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) ≠ 0  ##  ##  Model Details: ##   Number of regressors (k): 4 ##   Case: V  ##  ## ========================================

title: Introduction to kardl
text: narayan() function tailored small sample sizes. tests cointegration using critical values optimized small samples. Summary Narayan test provides detailed information test statistic, critical values, hypotheses, decision regarding cointegration.
code: test_result <- kardl_model |> narayan(case = 3, signif_level = "0.05") test_result ##  ##  Narayan F Test for Cointegration ##  ## data:  model ## F = 32.345 ## alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists summary(test_result) ##  ## ======================================== ## KARDL Cointegration Test Results ## ======================================== ##  ##  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 5% level) ##  ##  Test Statistic: ##   F: 32.3445539 ##  ##  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): ##           L     U ##   10% 3.160 4.230 ##   5%  3.678 4.840 ##   1%  4.890 6.164 ##  ##  ##  Comparison: ##   At the 5% significance level, F (32.3445539) exceeds the upper bound (4.84). ##   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. ##   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). ##  ##  Hypotheses: ## H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG) = Coef(L1.drivers_POS) = Coef(L1.drivers_NEG) = 0  ## H1: Not all of Coef(L1.DriversKilled), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG), Coef(L1.drivers_POS), Coef(L1.drivers_NEG) are zero.  ##  ##  Model Details: ##   Number of regressors (k): 4 ##   Case: V  ##  ##  ##  Note:The number of observations exceeds the maximum limit for the critical valuestable. Using the critical values for 80 observations. ## ========================================

title: Introduction to kardl
text: kardl(data, model, maxlag, mode, ...): data: time series dataset (e.g., data frame DriversKilled, PetrolPrice, drivers). asymmetric(): asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics. lasymmetric(): Long-run asymmetric variables. sasymmetric(): Short-run asymmetric variables. deterministic(): Fixed dummy variables. trend: Linear time trend. maxlag: Maximum number lags (default: 4). Use smaller values (e.g., 2) small datasets, larger values (e.g., 8) long-term dependencies. "quick": Verbose output interactive use. "grid": Verbose output lag optimization. "grid_custom": Silent, efficient execution. User-defined vector (e.g., c(1, 2, 4, 5) c(DriversKilled = 2, PetrolPrice_POS = 3, PetrolPrice_NEG = 1, drivers = 3)). Returns list components: inputs, finalModel, start_time, end_time, properLag, time_span, opt_lag, lag_criteria, type (“kardlmodel”). kardl_set(...): Configures options like criterion (AIC, BIC, AICc, HQ), different_asym_lag, asym_prefix, Sasymuffix, short_coef, long_coef. Use kardl_get() retrieve settings kardl_reset() restore defaults. kardl_longrun(model): Calculates standardized long-run coefficients, returning type (“kardl_longrun”), coef, delta_se, results, starsDesc. symmetrytest(model): Performs Wald tests short- long-run asymmetry, returning Lhypotheses, Lwald, Shypotheses, Swald, type (“symmetrytest”). pssf(model, case, signif_level): Performs Pesaran, Shin, Smith F Bound test cointegration, supporting cases 1–5 significance levels (“auto”, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.10). psst(model, case, signif_level): Performs PSS t Bound test, focusing lagged dependent variable’s coefficient. narayan(model, case, signif_level): Conducts Narayan test cointegration, optimized small samples (cases 2–5). ecm(data, model, maxlag, mode,  ...): Conducts Restricted ECM test cointegration, similar parameters kardl() case/significance level options. detailed documentation, use ?kardl, ?kardl_set, ?kardl_longrun, ?symmetrytest, ?pssf, ?psst, ?narayan, ?ecm.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: kardl package R tool estimating symmetric asymmetric Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) Nonlinear ARDL (NARDL) models, designed econometricians researchers analyzing cointegration dynamic relationships time series data. offers flexible model specifications, allowing users include deterministic variables, asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics, trend components. package supports customizable lag structures, model selection criteria (AIC, BIC, AICc, HQ), parallel processing computational efficiency. Key features include: Flexible Formula Specification: Use Asymmetric(), Lasymmetric(), Sasymmetric() model asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics, deterministic() dummy variables. Lag Optimization: Choose automatic lag selection ("quick", "grid", "grid_custom") user-defined lags. Dynamic Analysis: Compute long-run coefficients, perform cointegration tests (PSS F, PSS t, Narayan), ECM estimation. vignette demonstrates use kardl() function estimate asymmetric ARDL model, perform diagnostic tests, visualize results, using economic data Turkey.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: define model formula using R’s formula syntax, incorporating asymmetric effects deterministic variables. use asymmetric() variables short- long-run asymmetry, lasymmetric() long-run asymmetry, sasymmetric() short-run asymmetry, deterministic() fixed dummy variables. trend term includes linear time trend model. Indeed, formula syntax flexible, allowing various combinations asymmetric deterministic variables. following variations formula equivalent yield model specification:
code: # Define the model formula my_formula <- DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asymmetric(PetrolPrice + drivers) + deterministic(law) +   trend same_formula <- y ~ asymmetric(x1) +   sasymmetric(x2 + x3) +   lasymmetric(x4 + x5) +   deterministic(dummy1) + trend same_formula <- y ~ asymmetric(x1) +   sasymmetric(x2 + x3) +   lasymmetric(x4 + x5) +   deterministic(dummy1) + trend same_formula <- y ~ asym(x1) + sasym(x2 + x3) + lasym(x4 + x5) +   det(dummy1) + trend same_formula <- y ~ a(x1) + s(x2 + x3) + l(x4 + x5) + d(dummy1) + trend

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: lag_criteria component contains lag combinations criterion values. visualize compare model selection criteria (AIC, BIC, HQ).
code: library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(ggplot2) # Convert lag_criteria to a data frame lag_criteria <- as.data.frame(kardl_extract(kardl_model, "lag_criteria")) colnames(lag_criteria) <- c("lag", "AIC", "BIC", "AICc", "HQ") lag_criteria <- lag_criteria |> mutate(across(c(AIC, BIC, HQ), as.numeric))  # Pivot to long format lag_criteria_long <- lag_criteria |>   select(-AICc) |>   pivot_longer(     cols = c(AIC, BIC, HQ),     names_to = "Criteria",     values_to = "Value"   )  # Find minimum values min_values <- lag_criteria_long |>   group_by(Criteria) |>   slice_min(order_by = Value) |>   ungroup()  # Plot ggplot(   lag_criteria_long,   aes(x = lag, y = Value, color = Criteria, group = Criteria) ) +   geom_line() +   geom_point(     data = min_values, aes(x = lag, y = Value),     color = "red", size = 3, shape = 8   ) +   geom_text(     data = min_values, aes(x = lag, y = Value, label = lag),     vjust = 1.5, color = "black", size = 3.5   ) +   labs(     title = "Lag Criteria Comparison",     x = "Lag Configuration",     y = "Criteria Value"   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: data data frame time series object containing variables used model estimation. default value NULL, means user must provide dataset calling kardl() function.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: data parameter essential kardl() function perform model estimation. contain variables specified model formula, including dependent variable independent variables, asymmetric components, deterministic variables defined formula. trend generated automatically specified formula. Input data can form data frame, tibble, time series object (e.g., ts, xts, zoo). providing data, ensure : - dataset clean free missing values variables used model. - variables appropriately formatted (e.g., numeric continuous variables). - time series data ordered correctly, especially analysis involves lagged variables.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: formula formula object specifying model estimated. default value NULL, means user must provide model formula calling kardl() function.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: model parameter defines structure ARDL NARDL model estimated. include dependent variable left side formula independent variables, asymmetric components, deterministic variables, trend (applicable) right side. formula can include: - Asymmetric(): specify variables asymmetric effects short- long -run dynamics. - Lasymmetric(): specify variables asymmetric effects long-run -dynamics. - Sasymmetric(): specify variables asymmetric effects short-run -dynamics. - Deterministic(): include fixed dummy variables (e.g., seasonal d -ummies, event dummies). - trend: include linear time trend model. constructing model formula, ensure : - variables used formula present data provided. - formula syntactically correct follows R’s formula conventions. - use asymmetric deterministic functions appropriate research question data characteristics.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: maxlag integer value specifying maximum number lags considered model estimation. default value 4.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: maxlag parameter sets upper limit number lags kardl() function evaluate optimizing lag structure model. particularly important using modes like "grid" "grid_custom", function systematically tests different lag combinations specified maximum. choosing value maxlag, consider following: - Data Frequency: monthly data, maxlag 4 often sufficient capture short-term dynamics. quarterly data, lower maxlag ( e.g., 2) may appropriate, daily data, higher maxlag (e.g., 8 ) might necessary. - Sample Size: larger maxlag increases number parameters estimate, can problematic small sample sizes. Ensure sample size adequate support number lags considered. - Model Complexity: Higher maxlag values lead complex models, may overfit data. Balance need capturing dynamics risk overfitting. - Computational Resources: Evaluating large number lag combinations can computationally intensive. Consider available resources time constraints setting maxlag.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: mode character string numeric vector specifying mode model estimation. default value "quick". available options : - “quick”: mode provides fast estimation model without optimizing lags. suitable initial explorations user predefined lag structure. - “grid”: mode performs grid search possible lag combinations specified maxlag. provides verbose output, including lag criteria combination, useful thorough lag optimization. - “grid_custom”: Similar "grid", silent execution. efficient large datasets user wants avoid console output lag optimization process. - User-defined vector: user can specify custom lag structure providing numeric vector (e.g., c(1, 2, 4, 5)) named vector (e.g., c(DriversKilled = 2, PetrolPrice_POS = 3, PetrolPrice_NEG = 1, drivers = 3)). allows complete control lag selection process.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: mode parameter determines kardl() function approaches estimation ARDL NARDL model. mode advantages suited different scenarios: - Use "quick" rapid assessments lag structure already known computational speed priority. - Use "grid" comprehensive lag optimization, especially optimal lag structure unknown. mode ideal exploratory analysis model selection. - Use "grid_custom" efficient lag optimization without console output, particularly large datasets running multiple models batch mode. - Use user-defined vector user specific knowledge appropriate lags variable, allowing tailored model specifications. using "grid" "grid_custom", ensure maxlag parameter set appropriately balance thoroughness search computational feasibility.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: criterion character string specifying criterion used selecting optimal lags. default value "AIC". available options : User defined function: detailed information model selection criteria used methods, see documentation model_criterion function. AIC: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC). criterion balances model fit complexity, favoring models lower AIC value. widely used time series analysis model selection. BIC: Schwarz Criterion (SC), also known Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). criterion imposes stricter penalty model complexity compared AIC, often leading simpler models data size large. AICc: Corrected Akaike Information Criterion. modification AIC accounts small sample sizes. reliable AIC number observations limited. HQ: Hannan-Quinn Criterion. criterion similar AIC BIC uses logarithmic penalty term grows slowly BIC. often used econometric applications.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: detailed information model selection criteria used methods, see documentation model_criterion function. choice criterion can significantly impact selected lag length , consequently, performance model. criterion strengths suited specific scenarios: Use "AIC" general purposes, especially prioritizing good fit simplicity. Use "BIC" prefer parsimonious model, particularly large datasets. Use "AICc" working small sample sizes avoid overfitting. Use "HQ" balance AIC BIC, often econometrics time series models. Ensure selected criterion aligns goals analysis characteristics data.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: asym_prefix character vector specifying prefixes used naming asymmetric variables created positive negative decomposition. default value empty vector c(), indicating prefixes added default. specified, prefixes added beginning variable names represent positive negative decomposition: first element vector corresponds positive decomposition. second element vector corresponds negative decomposition.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: Asymmetric decomposition used analyze separate effects positive negative changes variable. example, given variable X, prefixes can used generate POS_X NEG_X positive negative components, respectively. default, prefixes applied (asym_prefix = c()). However, users can define custom prefixes providing vector two elements. example: kardl_set(asym_prefix = c("POS_", "NEG_")) results variable names POS_X NEG_X. kardl_set(asym_prefix = c("Increase_", "Decrease_")) results variable names Increase_X Decrease_X. Attention! vector must contain exactly two elements: first positive decomposition second negative decomposition. prefixes used combination suffixes (via asym_suffix), ensure resulting variable names meaningful conflict.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: order suffixes important: first element vector corresponds positive decomposition. second element vector corresponds negative decomposition. Asymmetric decomposition commonly used models separate effects positive negative changes variable. example, given variable X, decomposition may result X_POS X_NEG represent positive negative components, respectively. default, suffixes "_POS" "_NEG" used, users can customize needed providing custom vector. example: asym_suffix = c("_Increase", "_Decrease") results variable names X_Increase X_Decrease. asym_suffix = c("_Up", "_Down") results variable names X_Up X_Down. Attention! first element must always represent positive decomposition. second element must always represent negative decomposition. Providing fewer elements incorrect ordering may cause errors variable naming.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: batch parameter particularly useful dealing large datasets complex models require significant computational resources. specifying batch size, users can divide model estimation process smaller, manageable segments, can processed parallel. format batch parameter "m/n", : m number batches processed parallel. n total number batches. example, setting batch = "2/4" divide estimation 4 batches, 2 batches processed simultaneously. can significantly reduce computation time, especially models extensive lag structures large numbers variables. Attention! Ensure specified batch size appropriate available computational resources (e.g., CPU cores, memory). Overloading system many parallel processes may lead performance degradation failures. effectiveness batching may vary depending specific model dataset characteristics.

text: null alternative hypotheses restricted ECM test follows: $$\mathbf{H_{0}:} \theta  =   0$$ $$\mathbf{H_{1}:} \theta  \neq 0$$ null hypothesis (\(H_{0}\)) states cointegration model, meaning long-run relationship variables significant. alternative hypothesis (\(H_{1}\)) suggests cointegration, indicating significant long-term relationship variables. test statistic calculated t-statistic coefficient error correction term (\(\theta\)) ECM model. absolute value t-statistic exceeds critical value PSS t Bound table, reject null hypothesis favor alternative hypothesis, indicating cointegration present. cases restricted ECM Bound test defined follows: case 1: constant, trend. case used model include constant term trend term. suitable models variables stationary exhibit long-term trends. model specified follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta y_t =  \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} + \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} + \theta (y_{t-1}  - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t \end{aligned} $$ case 2: Restricted constant, trend. case used model includes constant term trend term. suitable models variables exhibit long-term relationship trend component. model specified follows: $$   \begin{aligned}   \Delta y_t &= \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} +   \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} +   \theta (y_{t-1} - \alpha_0 - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t   \end{aligned}   $$ case 3: Unrestricted constant, trend. case used model includes unrestricted constant term trend term. suitable models variables exhibit long-term relationship constant trend component. model specified follows: $$   \begin{aligned}   \Delta y_t &= \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} +   \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} +   \theta (y_{t-1} - \alpha_0 - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t   \end{aligned}     $$ case 4: Unrestricted Constant, restricted trend. case used model includes unrestricted constant term restricted trend term. suitable models variables exhibit long-term relationship constant trend component. model specified follows: $$   \begin{aligned}   \Delta y_t &= \phi + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} +   \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} +   \theta (y_{t-1} - \pi (t-1) - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t   \end{aligned}    $$ case 5: Unrestricted constant, unrestricted trend. Error Correction Model (ECM) specified follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta y_t &= \phi + \varphi t + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} + \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} + \theta (y_{t-1} - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t \end{aligned} $$

what: kardl: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations — kardl-package
title: kardl: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations — kardl-package
text: Implements estimation procedures Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) Nonlinear ARDL (NARDL) models, allow researchers investigate short- long-run relationships time series data mixed orders integration. package supports simultaneous modeling symmetric asymmetric regressors, flexible treatment short-run long-run asymmetries, automated equation handling. includes several cointegration testing approaches Pesaran-Shin-Smith F t bounds tests, narayan test. Methodological foundations provided Pesaran, Shin, Smith (2001) doi:10.1016/S0304-4076(01)00049-5  Shin, Yu, Greenwood-Nimmo (2014, ISBN:9780123855079).

text: general formula long-run model specified follows: $$ \begin{aligned}  \Delta {y}_t =  c  +  \eta _0   {y}_{t-1} + \sum_{j=1}^{p}  { \gamma_{j}  \Delta {y}_{t-j} }   +\sum_{=1}^{m}  {( \eta ^{+}_i   {x}^{+}_{,t-1 } + \eta ^{-}_i   {x}^{-}_{,t-1 } ) }  +  \sum_{=m+1}^{k} {\eta _i   {x}_{,t-1 } } +  \newline  \sum_{=1}^{m} {\sum_{j=0}^{q^+_i} { \beta^+_{ij}   \Delta {x}^+_{,t-j} } } + \sum_{=1}^{m} {\sum_{j=0}^{q^-_i} { \beta^-_{ij}   \Delta {x}^-_{,t-j} } } + \sum_{=m+1}^{k} {\sum_{j=0}^{q_i} { \beta_{ij}   \Delta {x}_{,t-j} } } + e_t \end{aligned}  $$ : \(y_t\) dependent variable time t. \(c\) constant term. \(\eta_0\) coefficient lagged dependent variable. \(\gamma_j\) coefficients lagged differences dependent variable. \(\eta^+_i\) \(\eta^-_i\) coefficients positive negative decompositions independent variables, respectively. \(\eta_i\) coefficients independent variables asymmetric decompositions. \(\beta^+_{ij}\) \(\beta^-_{ij}\) coefficients lagged differences positive negative decompositions independent variables, respectively. \(\beta_{ij}\) coefficients lagged differences independent variables asymmetric decompositions. \(e_t\) error term time t. \(p\) maximum lag length dependent variable. \(q^+_i\) \(q^-_i\) maximum lag lengths positive negative decompositions independent variables, respectively. \(q_i\) maximum lag length independent variables asymmetric decompositions. \(m\) number independent variables asymmetric decompositions. \(k\) total number independent variables. \(\Delta\) denotes first difference operator. \(x^+_{,t}\) \(x^-_{,t}\) represent positive negative decompositions independent variable \(x_i\) time t, respectively.

what: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: kardl_extract() generic accessor retrieving selected documented components objects produced kardl package.

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
code: kardl_extract(kardl_object, what, variable = NULL, component = NULL)

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: kardl_object supported object produced kardl package. character string specifying component extract. available options depend class object documented sections . variable optional character string specifying particular variable extract results , applicable. available options depend selected value documented relevant sections . argument available just kardl_symmetric objects, users can extract results specific variables included symmetry test. example, symmetry test includes variables "drivers" "PetrolPrice", users can specify variable = "drivers" extract results related "drivers" variable, variable = c("drivers", "PetrolPrice") extract results variables. variable specified extracting components include variable-specific results, function return results variables included symmetry test. component optional character string specifying particular component extract, applicable. argument available just kardl_symmetric objects, users can specify whether want extract long-run short-run results symmetry test. example, set "long_wald_tests", users can specify component = "longrun" extract long-run Wald test results, component = "H0" extract long-run null hypothesis description. component specified extracting components include multiple subcomponents (e.g., long-run short-run results), function return available subcomponents selected value.

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: requested component. returned object depends class object selected value .

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: provides stable user-facing interface accessing important results without requiring users rely internal list structure returned objects.

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: kardl_extract() currently supports: kardl_lm kardl_mplier kardl_boot kardl_test kardl_test_summary kardl_symmetric

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: fitted kardl_lm model objects returned kardl(), may one : data_ts_info:  time-series information input data. data_is_ts:  whether input data time series. data_class:  class input data. data_start:  starting time input data. data_end:  ending time input data. data_frequency:  frequency input data. data_deltat:  delta time input data. data_tsp:  time-series properties input data. data_time:  time index input data. no_constant:  Whether model excludes intercept. trend:  Trend specification. asym_long_vars:  Variables long-run asymmetry. asym_short_vars:  Variables short-run asymmetry. deterministic:  Deterministic regressors. dependent_var:  Dependent variable. independent_vars:  Independent variables. all_vars:  variables used model. all_asym_vars:  asymmetric variables. indep_as_excluded:  Short-run asymmetric variables excluded linear set. indep_al_excluded:  Long-run asymmetric variables excluded linear set. short_run_vars:  Short-run variables. long_run_vars:  Long-run variables. shortrun_length:  Number short-run terms. lag_rows_number:  Number rows lost due lag construction. model_type:  Model type. data:  Prepared data used internally. start_time:  Starting time estimation sample. end_time:  Ending time estimation sample. span:  Time span estimation sample. opt_lag:  Selected optimal lag order. lag_criteria:  Lag-selection criterion values. all_cr_lags:  Candidate lag combinations criterion values. k:  Number regressors used relevant post-estimation procedures. n:  Effective sample size.

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: dynamic multiplier objects returned mplier(), may one : multipliers: Estimated dynamic multipliers. omega:  Adjustment-related multiplier matrix. lambda:  Short-run coefficient matrix used multiplier construction. vars:  Variables included multiplier calculation. horizon:  Multiplier horizon.

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: bootstrapped multiplier objects returned bootstrap(), may one : multipliers:  Bootstrapped dynamic multiplier results. level:  Confidence level used bootstrap intervals. replications:  Number bootstrap replications. vars:  Variables included bootstrap procedure. horizon:  Multiplier horizon.

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: kardl_test class includes objects returned pssf(), psst(), narayan() test objects, may one : type:  Type test. case:  Deterministic case used test. statistic:  Test statistic. method:  Test method description. alternative:  Alternative hypothesis. data.name:  Name data model object. sample.size:  Effective sample size. hypotheses:  Textual description null alternative hypotheses. var_names:  Variables involved test. k:  Number regressors entering bounds test. n:  Sample size, available. sig:  Significance-level information. notes:  Additional notes.

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: kardl_test_summary class includes summary objects produced summary() methods kardl_test objects, produced summary.pssf(), summary.psst(), summary.narayan(). test summary objects, may one : statistic:  Test statistic. case:  Textual description deterministic case. variables:  Variables included tested restriction. decision:  Textual test decision. hypotheses:  Textual description null alternative hypotheses. numeric_decision:  Numeric encoding test decision. significance_level:  Significance level used decision. critical_values:  Lower upper critical-value bounds. k:  Number regressors entering bounds test. notes:  Additional notes.

title: Extract Components from kardl Objects — kardl_extract
text: kardl_symmetric class includes objects returned symmetrytest() function, tests long-run short-run asymmetry models fitted kardl() include asymmetric terms. symmetry test objects, may one : long_wald_summary:  Summary long-run Wald test results. long_hypotheses:  Textual description long-run null alternative hypotheses. short_wald_summary:  Summary short-run Wald test results. short_hypotheses:  Textual description short-run null alternative hypotheses. long_wald_tests:  Detailed long-run Wald test results. short_wald_tests:  Detailed short-run Wald test results. vars:  Variables included symmetry test. type:  Type symmetry test. call:  Original function call produced object. component variable arguments allow subsetting symmetry test results, enabling users extract specific components (long-run short-run) /results specific variables interest.

text: object class kardl_long_run, list containing: coefficients: named vector long-run multipliers. residuals: vector residuals long-run model. effects: vector effects long-run model. rank: rank long-run model. fitted.values: vector fitted values long-run model. assign: vector indicating assignment coefficients terms long-run model. qr: QR decomposition design matrix long-run model. df.residual: degrees freedom residuals long-run model. xlevels: list factor levels used long-run model. call: matched call used create long-run model. terms: terms object long-run model. model: data frame used long-run model.

title: Compute Long-Run Multipliers from a kardl Model — kardl_longrun
text: function also calculates standard errors long-run multipliers using delta method, accounts covariance coefficients. fitted values residuals long-run model calculated based original data long-run multipliers. function returns object class kardl_long_run, contains long-run multipliers, standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, fitted values, residuals, relevant information analysis diagnostics. Note fitted values residuals long-run model centered (.e., mean zero) design, means diagnostic plots residual-based tests may valid model. primary focus function estimation long-run multipliers associated statistics. long-run multipliers calculated using formula: $$LRM_i = -\frac{\eta_i}{\eta_0}$$. t-values p-values calculated using standard errors obtained delta method, accounts covariance coefficients. Delta method formula standard errors long-run multipliers: $$SE(LR_i) = \sqrt{(^2) \cdot Var(\eta_i) + 2 \cdot \cdot B \cdot Cov(\eta_i, \eta_0) + (B^2) \cdot Var(\eta_0)}$$ $$= \frac{\partial LRM_i}{\partial \eta_i} = -\frac{1}{\eta_0}$$ $$B = \frac{\partial LRM_i}{\partial \eta_0} = \frac{\eta_i}{\eta_0^2}$$. Hence, \(\eta_i\) coefficient independent variable \(\eta_0\) coefficient dependent variable original KARDL model.

title: Model Selection Criteria — model_criterion
text: function returns model selection criteria used compare quality different models. criteria defined lower values indicate better models (.e., goal minimization). wish compare models using maximization approach (e.g., log-likelihood), can multiply result -1. Note: predefined string options (e.g., "AIC") built-R functions AIC() BIC(). particular, values returned function adjusted dividing sample size n (.e., normalized AIC/BIC), makes comparable across datasets different sizes. function returns: "AIC":  \( \frac{2k - 2\ell}{n} \) Akaike Information Criterion divided n. "BIC":  \( \frac{\log(n) \cdot k - 2\ell}{n} \) Bayesian Information Criterion divided n. "AICc": \( \frac{2k(k+1)}{n - k - 1} +  \frac{2k - 2\ell}{n} \) Corrected Akaike Information Criterion divided n. "HQ": \( \frac{2 \log(\log(n)) \cdot k - 2\ell}{n} \) Hannan-Quinn Criterion divided n. "AdjR2": Adjusted R-squared, computed \(1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)(n - 1)}{n - k - 1}\). case, higher values indicate better models, function still returns negative adjusted R-squared consistency minimization approach criteria. : \(k\) number parameters, \(n\) sample size, \(\ell\) log-likelihood model. cr function, called fitted model additional arguments passed ....

code: # Example usage of model_criterion function with a simple linear model mylm <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars) model_criterion(mylm, AIC) #> [1] 156.6523 model_criterion(mylm, "AIC") #> 'log Lik.' 4.832886 (df=4)  # Example usage of model_criterion function with a kardl model kardl_model <- kardl(   DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice) +     deterministic(law) + trend,   Seatbelts,   mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0) )  # Using AIC as the kardl package's built-in criterion function which is # different from the base R AIC function. model_criterion(kardl_model, "AIC") #> 'log Lik.' 7.802762 (df=17)  # Using the base R AIC function directly on the fitted model object model_criterion(kardl_model, AIC) #> [1] 1461.117 # Using the base R AIC function outside of model_criterion to compute AIC for #  the fitted model AIC(kardl_model) #> [1] 1461.117  # Using BIC as the criterion for the kardl model which is different from the # base R BIC function. model_criterion(kardl_model, "BIC") #> 'log Lik.' 8.079221 (df=17)  # Using a custom criterion function that divides AIC by the sample size my_cr_fun <- function(mod, ...) {   AIC(mod) / length(mod$model[[1]]) } model_criterion(kardl_model, my_cr_fun) #> [1] 7.813457

title: Compute Dynamic Multipliers for KARDL Models — mplier
text: asymmetry structure determined internally: Variables extracted_info$asym_short_vars treated asymmetric short run. Variables extracted_info$asym_long_vars treated asymmetric long run. allows four possible configurations: LL: Linear short-run long-run NN: Asymmetric short-run long-run SA: Short-run linear, long-run asymmetric : Short-run asymmetric, long-run linear component linear, coefficient path used positive negative changes. asymmetric, separate positive negative effects computed. mplier function computes dynamic multipliers based coefficients lag structure model estimated using kardl package. function extracts necessary information model, coefficients, lag structure, variable names, compute dynamic multipliers. calculates short-run coefficients, Lambda values, omega values based model's parameters lag structure. output includes matrix dynamic multipliers (mpsi), can used analysis visualization. dynamic multipliers provide insight changes independent variables affect dependent variable time, allowing deeper understanding relationships captured model. function also allows users set minimum p-value threshold including coefficients calculation, providing flexibility focusing statistically significant effects. function constructs dynamic multipliers based recursive relationship: $$ \psi_{h}^{+} = \sum_{=0}^{h} \frac{\partial y_{t+}}{\partial x_{t}^{+}}, \quad \psi_{h}^{-} = \sum_{=0}^{h} \frac{\partial y_{t+}}{\partial x_{t}^{-}} $$ \(\psi_h^{+}\) \(\psi_h^{-}\) represent cumulative responses positive negative shocks. recursion defined : $$ \psi_h = \lambda_h + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \omega_j \psi_{h-j} $$ \(\lambda_h\) captures short-run effects \(\omega_j\) reflects persistence lagged dependent variables. asymmetry present, positive negative shocks propagated separately. Otherwise, dynamic path used.

title: Compute Dynamic Multipliers for KARDL Models — mplier
code: # This example demonstrates how to use the mplier function to calculate # dynamic multipliers from a model estimated using the kardl package. # The example includes fitting a model with the kardl function, # calculating the multipliers, and visualizing the results using both # base R plotting and ggplot2.  # Calculating dynamic multipliers for a linear model in short and long run # (NN)  kardl_model <- kardl(DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice, Seatbelts) m <- mplier(kardl_model, 40) head(m$mpsi) #>      h PetrolPrice_POS PetrolPrice_NEG PetrolPrice_dif #> [1,] 0       -490.8080        490.8080               0 #> [2,] 1       -721.1955        721.1955               0 #> [3,] 2       -794.5612        794.5612               0 #> [4,] 3       -806.3901        806.3901               0 #> [5,] 4       -802.6589        802.6589               0 #> [6,] 5       -798.3919        798.3919               0 plot(m)   # Calculating dynamic multipliers for a model with # Short-run linear, long-run asymmetric (SA) kardl_model <- kardl(DriversKilled ~ lasym(PetrolPrice), Seatbelts) m <- mplier(kardl_model, horizon = 40, min_prob = 0) head(kardl_extract(m, "multipliers")) #>      h PetrolPrice_POS PetrolPrice_NEG PetrolPrice_dif #> [1,] 0       -395.0500        395.0500               0 #> [2,] 1       -593.0817        593.0817               0 #> [3,] 2       -668.0545        668.0545               0 #> [4,] 3       -659.7015        659.7015               0 #> [5,] 4       -593.6678        593.6678               0 #> [6,] 5       -531.0524        531.0524               0 plot(m)   # Calculating dynamic multipliers for a model with # Short-run asymmetric, long-run linear (AS) kardl_model <- kardl(DriversKilled ~ sasym(PetrolPrice), Seatbelts) m <- mplier(kardl_model, 40) plot(m)   # Calculating dynamic multipliers for a model with # asymmetric effects in both short and long run (NN) kardl_model <- kardl(DriversKilled ~ asym(PetrolPrice) + drivers, Seatbelts) m <- mplier(kardl_model, 40) plot(m) #> Warning: Multiple variables selected. Only the first one will be plotted.   # The multipliers matrix contains the cumulative dynamic multipliers for each # variable and time horizon. The omega vector contains the persistence # structure of the model, while the lambda matrix contains the short-run # dynamic coefficients. You can inspect these components to understand the # dynamics captured by the model.  head(kardl_extract(m, "multipliers")) #>      h drivers_POS drivers_NEG drivers_dif PetrolPrice_POS PetrolPrice_NEG #> [1,] 0  0.07814436 -0.07814436           0     -283.148538    -1172.694364 #> [2,] 1  0.08894958 -0.08894958           0      -24.694623        8.116859 #> [3,] 2  0.07452635 -0.07452635           0       -6.876406       84.849854 #> [4,] 3  0.07372508 -0.07372508           0      -16.390854       41.256389 #> [5,] 4  0.07426117 -0.07426117           0      -17.332576       37.131221 #> [6,] 5  0.07430640 -0.07430640           0      -16.989924       38.706236 #>      PetrolPrice_dif #> [1,]     -1455.84290 #> [2,]       -16.57776 #> [3,]        77.97345 #> [4,]        24.86554 #> [5,]        19.79864 #> [6,]        21.71631 head(kardl_extract(m, "omega")) #> [1]  0.02660202 -0.03864692 head(kardl_extract(m, "lambda")) #>       drivers_POS  drivers_NEG PetrolPrice_POS PetrolPrice_NEG #> [1,]  0.078144356  0.078144356       -283.1485        1172.694 #> [2,]  0.008726426  0.008726426        265.9862       -1212.007 #> [3,] -0.011690630 -0.011690630          0.0000           0.000 #> [4,]  0.000000000  0.000000000          0.0000           0.000 #> [5,]  0.000000000  0.000000000          0.0000           0.000 #> [6,]  0.000000000  0.000000000          0.0000           0.000  # For plotting specific variables, you can specify them in the plot # function. For example, to plot the multipliers for the variable "drivers":  plot(m, variable = "drivers", title = "Dynamic Multipliers for drivers")

title: Narayan Bounds Test — narayan
text: function performs Narayan test, designed assess cointegration using critical values specifically tailored small sample sizes. Unlike traditional cointegration tests may rely asymptotic distributions, Narayan test adjusts limitations small samples, providing accurate results contexts. makes test particularly useful studies fewer observations, accounts sample size constraints determining presence long-term equilibrium relationship variables.

title: Narayan Bounds Test — narayan
code: kardl_model<-kardl(                    DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice)+                        deterministic(law)+trend,                    Seatbelts,                    mode=c(1,2,3,0)) my_test<-narayan(kardl_model, case=3, signif_level="auto") # Getting the results of the test. my_test #>  #> 	Narayan F Test for Cointegration #>  #> data:  model #> F = 39.508 #> alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists #>  # Getting details of the test. my_summary<-summary(my_test) my_summary #>  #> ======================================== #> KARDL Cointegration Test Results #> ======================================== #>  #>  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 1% level) #>  #>  Test Statistic: #>   F: 39.5080601 #>  #>  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): #>           L     U #>   10% 3.588 4.605 #>   5%  4.203 5.320 #>   1%  5.620 6.908 #>  #>  #>  Comparison: #>   At the 1% significance level, F (39.5080601) exceeds the upper bound (6.908). #>   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. #>   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). #>  #>  Hypotheses: #> H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG) = Coef(L1.drivers) = 0  #> H1: Not all of Coef(L1.DriversKilled), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG), Coef(L1.drivers) are zero.  #>  #>  Model Details: #>   Number of regressors (k): 3 #>   Case: V  #>  #>  #>  Note:The number of observations exceeds the maximum limit for the critical valuestable. Using the critical values for 80 observations. #> ========================================  # Getting the critical values of the test. kardl_extract(my_summary, what = "critical_values") #>          L     U #> 0.10 3.588 4.605 #> 0.05 4.203 5.320 #> 0.01 5.620 6.908     # Using magrittr :  library(magrittr) Seatbelts %>%   kardl(     DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice) +       deterministic(law) + trend,     mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0), data = .   ) %>%   narayan() #>  #> 	Narayan F Test for Cointegration #>  #> data:  model #> F = 39.508 #> alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists #>   # Getting details of the test results using magrittr: Seatbelts %>%   kardl(     DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice) +       deterministic(law) + trend,     mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0), data = .   ) %>%   narayan() %>%   summary() #>  #> ======================================== #> KARDL Cointegration Test Results #> ======================================== #>  #>  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 1% level) #>  #>  Test Statistic: #>   F: 39.5080601 #>  #>  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): #>           L     U #>   10% 3.588 4.605 #>   5%  4.203 5.320 #>   1%  5.620 6.908 #>  #>  #>  Comparison: #>   At the 1% significance level, F (39.5080601) exceeds the upper bound (6.908). #>   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. #>   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). #>  #>  Hypotheses: #> H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG) = Coef(L1.drivers) = 0  #> H1: Not all of Coef(L1.DriversKilled), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG), Coef(L1.drivers) are zero.  #>  #>  Model Details: #>   Number of regressors (k): 3 #>   Case: V  #>  #>  #>  Note:The number of observations exceeds the maximum limit for the critical valuestable. Using the critical values for 80 observations. #> ========================================

code: kardl_model <- kardl(   DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice) +     deterministic(law) + trend,   Seatbelts,   mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0) ) my_pssF <- pssf(kardl_model, case = "auto", signif_level = "auto") # Getting the results of the test. my_pssF #>  #> 	Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds F-test for cointegration #>  #> data:  kardl_model #> F = 39.508 #> alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists #>  # Getting details of the test. my_summary <- summary(my_pssF) my_summary #>  #> ======================================== #> KARDL Cointegration Test Results #> ======================================== #>  #>  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 1% level) #>  #>  Test Statistic: #>   F: 39.5080601 #>  #>  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): #>           L    U #>   10%  3.47 4.45 #>   5%   4.01 5.07 #>   2.5% 4.52 5.62 #>   1%   5.17 6.36 #>  #>  #>  Comparison: #>   At the 1% significance level, F (39.5080601) exceeds the upper bound (6.36). #>   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. #>   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). #>  #>  Hypotheses: #> H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG) = Coef(L1.drivers) = 0  #> H1: Not all of Coef(L1.DriversKilled), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG), Coef(L1.drivers) are zero.  #>  #>  Model Details: #>   Number of regressors (k): 3 #>   Case: V  #>  #> ========================================  # Getting the critical values of the test. kardl_extract(my_summary, what = "critical_values") #>          L    U #> 0.10  3.47 4.45 #> 0.05  4.01 5.07 #> 0.025 4.52 5.62 #> 0.01  5.17 6.36  # Using magrittr :  library(magrittr) Seatbelts %>%   kardl(     DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice) +       deterministic(law) + trend,     mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0), data = .   ) %>%   pssf() #>  #> 	Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds F-test for cointegration #>  #> data:  . #> F = 39.508 #> alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists #>   # Getting details of the test results using magrittr: Seatbelts %>%   kardl(     DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice) +       deterministic(law) + trend,     mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0), data = .   ) %>%   pssf() %>%   summary() #>  #> ======================================== #> KARDL Cointegration Test Results #> ======================================== #>  #>  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 1% level) #>  #>  Test Statistic: #>   F: 39.5080601 #>  #>  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): #>           L    U #>   10%  3.47 4.45 #>   5%   4.01 5.07 #>   2.5% 4.52 5.62 #>   1%   5.17 6.36 #>  #>  #>  Comparison: #>   At the 1% significance level, F (39.5080601) exceeds the upper bound (6.36). #>   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. #>   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). #>  #>  Hypotheses: #> H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS) = Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG) = Coef(L1.drivers) = 0  #> H1: Not all of Coef(L1.DriversKilled), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_POS), Coef(L1.PetrolPrice_NEG), Coef(L1.drivers) are zero.  #>  #>  Model Details: #>   Number of regressors (k): 3 #>   Case: V  #>  #> ========================================

title: Pesaran, Shin, and Smith t Bounds Test — psst
text: function performs Pesaran, Shin, Smith (PSS) t Bound test, used detect existence long-term relationship (cointegration) variables autoregressive distributed lag (ARDL) model. t Bound test specifically focuses significance coefficient lagged dependent variable, helping assess whether variable reverts long-term equilibrium short-term deviations. test provides critical values upper lower bounds. t-statistic falls within appropriate range, confirms presence cointegration. test particularly useful working datasets containing stationary non-stationary variables.

title: Pesaran, Shin, and Smith t Bounds Test — psst
text: PSS t Bound test evaluates null hypothesis long-run coefficients model equal zero alternative hypothesis least one non-zero. test conducted different cases, depending model specification. $$   \Delta {y}_t =  \psi  + \varphi t  + \eta _0   {y}_{t-1}  +   \sum_{=1}^{k} {  \eta _i   {x}_{,t-1} }  +   \sum_{j=1}^{p} { \gamma_{j}  \Delta {y}_{t-j} }+   \sum_{=1}^{k} {\sum_{j=0}^{q_i} { \beta_{ij}   \Delta {x}_{,t-j} } }+ e_t $$ $$\mathbf{H_{0}:} \eta_0   = 0$$ $$\mathbf{H_{1}:} \eta_{0}  \neq 0$$

title: Pesaran, Shin, and Smith t Bounds Test — psst

title: Pesaran, Shin, and Smith t Bounds Test — psst
code: kardl_model<-kardl(                    DriversKilled~PetrolPrice+drivers+asym(PetrolPrice)+                          deterministic(law)+trend,                    Seatbelts,                    mode=c(1,2,3,0)) my_test<-psst(kardl_model) # Getting the results of the test. my_test #>  #> 	Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds t-test for cointegration #>  #> data:  model #> t = -12.553 #> alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists #>  # Getting details of the test. my_summary<-summary(my_test) my_summary #>  #> ======================================== #> KARDL Cointegration Test Results #> ======================================== #>  #>  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 1% level) #>  #>  Test Statistic: #>   t: -12.5530733 #>  #>  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): #>            L     U #>   10%  -3.13 -3.84 #>   5%   -3.41 -4.16 #>   2.5% -3.65 -4.42 #>   1%   -3.96 -4.73 #>  #>  #>  Comparison: #>   At the 1% significance level, t (12.5530733) exceeds the upper bound (4.73). #>   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. #>   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). #>  #>  Hypotheses: #> H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = 0  #> H1: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) ≠ 0  #>  #>  Model Details: #>   Number of regressors (k): 3 #>   Case: V  #>  #> ========================================  # Getting the critical values of the test. kardl_extract(my_summary, what = "critical_values") #>           L     U #> 0.10  -3.13 -3.84 #> 0.05  -3.41 -4.16 #> 0.025 -3.65 -4.42 #> 0.01  -3.96 -4.73  # Using a robust variance-covariance matrix for the test: if (requireNamespace("sandwich", quietly = TRUE)) {     vcov_matrix <- sandwich::vcovHC(kardl_model)     my_test_robust <- psst(kardl_model,                            case = 1,                            signif_level =0.01,                            vcov = vcov_matrix)   my_test_robust } #>  #> 	Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds t-test for cointegration #>  #> data:  model #> t = -12.442 #> alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists #>    # Using magrittr :  library(magrittr) Seatbelts %>%   kardl(     DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice) +       deterministic(law) + trend,     mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0), data = .   ) %>%   psst() #>  #> 	Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds t-test for cointegration #>  #> data:  model #> t = -12.553 #> alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists #>   # Getting details of the test results using magrittr: Seatbelts %>%   kardl(     DriversKilled ~ PetrolPrice + drivers + asym(PetrolPrice) +       deterministic(law) + trend,     mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0), data = .   ) %>%   psst() %>%   summary() #>  #> ======================================== #> KARDL Cointegration Test Results #> ======================================== #>  #>  Decision: Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 1% level) #>  #>  Test Statistic: #>   t: -12.5530733 #>  #>  Critical Values (Lower & Upper Bounds): #>            L     U #>   10%  -3.13 -3.84 #>   5%   -3.41 -4.16 #>   2.5% -3.65 -4.42 #>   1%   -3.96 -4.73 #>  #>  #>  Comparison: #>   At the 1% significance level, t (12.5530733) exceeds the upper bound (4.73). #>   This indicates that the variables tend to move together over  time. #>   Conclusion: There is strong evidence of a long-run relationship  (cointegration). #>  #>  Hypotheses: #> H0: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) = 0  #> H1: Coef(L1.DriversKilled) ≠ 0  #>  #>  Model Details: #>   Number of regressors (k): 3 #>   Case: V  #>  #> ========================================
